title: Firm Performance Pay as Insurance against Promotion Risk
abstract: The prevalence of pay based on risky firm outcomes for nonexecutive workers presents a puzzling departure from conventional contract theory, which predicts insurance provision by the firm. When workers at the same firm compete against each other for promotions, the optimal contract features pay based on firm outcomes as insurance against promotion risk. The model's predictions are consistent with many observed phenomena, such as performance-based vesting and overvaluation of equity pay by nonexecutive workers. It also generates novel predictions linking a firm's hierarchy to its workers' pay structure.

article.introduction:
The moral hazard literature suggeststhat firms should insure risk-averse workers against uncertain firm outcomes that are beyond their individual control. Yet, nonexecutive workers in the United States routinely receive performance pay based on uncertain firm outcomes (Kruse, Blasi, and Park (2010)). As Bergman and Jenter (2007, p. 668) point out, the prevalence of such performance pay for nonexecutive workers “is a puzzle for standard economic theory: any positive incentive effects should be diminished by free-rider problems and overshadowed by the cost of imposing risk on employees.” This paper argues that when workers compete against each other for promotions, performance pay based on firm outcomes can insure against poor promotion prospects. In such a setting, performance pay based on firm outcomes is not indicative of inefficient risk-sharing; rather, itisa form of insurance.
  Promotion tournaments are common in the workplace. When workers compete against each other for promotions, they facepromotion risk—the possibility of being passed over for promotion despite performing well. Cowgill (2015) reports that77.4%$77.4\%$of 16,377 respondents to a Google Consumer Survey say that there are a “limited number of promotion slots [at their workplace], even if all workers perform well.” Consistent with this view, DeVaro (2006) reports that workers are less likely to be promoted when the typical performance of their coworkers is higher.1Using the quasi-random assignment of talent at a large technology and services firm, Cowgill (2015) shows that workers have lower job satisfaction when they face more promotion competition. This body of evidence suggests that promotion risk is a relevant source of uncertainty for many workers in the economy.
  Better firm outcomes correspond to a larger pool of high-performing workers and, hence, to more intense competition for promotion and worse promotion prospects for an individual worker. The firm can insure workers against promotion risk with firm performance pay—bonuses that increase with both individual performance and firm outcome. This insurance motive results in an optimal contract with features consistent with common compensation practices such as profit-sharing for high-performing workers. The insurance role of firm performance pay also sheds some light on phenomena such as the overvaluation of stock option pay by nonexecutive workers and generates novel predictions linking the firm's hierarchy to its workers' pay structure.
  I study the insurance role of firm performance pay in a parsimonious two-period model in which a risk-neutral firm enters into one-period contracts with many risk-averse workers. Production at the firm involves two types of tasks: lower level and higher level. A worker's output in these tasks depends on that worker's effort and type, which can be high or low and is ex ante unknown to all. In the first period, all workers engage in lower level tasks. Only high-type workers who exert effort generate high output in lower level tasks. In the second period, the firm has higher level tasks that require high-type workers, motivating it to promote only workers with high output in lower level tasks. The possibility of a promotion to a higher level task with better pay in the second period provides implicit incentives to workers engaging in lower level tasks in the first period. However, workers are exposed to promotion risk because the number of higher level tasks is limited.
  The insurance role of firm performance pay relies on how workers' preferences over pay respond to news. In particular, the arrival of bad news about future promotion prospects, and hence future income, increases the marginal utility of current pay. Such a preference arises naturally in many dynamic choice settings. In the model, workers face a consumption-smoothing problem. Borrowing for first-period consumption is more costly when promotion risk is high; doing so likely involves second-period repayment when income is low due to missing out on a promotion. As a result, workers derive an additional benefit from pay when promotion risk is high.
  This paper highlights an underexplored tension in the employment relationship. When more workers at a firm perform well, the firm does better. At the same time, better overall worker performance raises the bar for promotion.2Consistent with this tension, Phillips (2001) studies the 50-year history of Silicon Valley law firms and their attorneys and documents a “promotion paradox,” that is, a negative relationship between an associate's likelihood of promotion to partner and firm strength.3
  Large professional services firms provide a natural setting to apply the paper's results. First, professional workers in this setting face some variation of the promotion-to-partner tournament known as the “Cravath System,” named after the prestigious U.S. law firm that popularized its use (Malhotra, Morris, and Smets (2010)). In a promotion-to-partner tournament, associates compete over a period of time for the prize of partnership, which is awarded to a fixed percentage of the associates (Galanter and Palay (1999)). Frictions such as decreasing marginal returns of the production technology, limited monitoring capacity at the top (Oi (1983)), and the need to signal quality (Levin and Tadelis (2005)) constrain the firm's ability to accommodate talent by increasing the number of partner positions.4Consistent with such organizational frictions, the ratio of associates to partners at large professional services firms tends to increase with firm size.5Second, professional services firms rely on their workers' human capital to generate output. A strong correlation between the firm outcome (e.g., total revenue) and aggregate worker performance (e.g., total billable hours) makes the outcome a more precise signal of promotion prospects, lowering the basis risk of using firm performance pay to insure against promotion risk. Consistent with the model's predictions, large professional services firms routinely pay their associates bonuses that are contingent on the associate generating a certain number of billable hours and the firm achieving a profitability target. The model's results also apply to mid-level workers in large, knowledge-intensive firms, which face significant organizational frictions and rely on their workers' human capital to generate output.
  The insurance role of firm performance pay may help explain some puzzling empirical findings. First, most workers prefer to have some exposure to firm outcomes in their compensation (Kruse, Blasi, and Park (2010)). Many voluntarily acquire equity in their employer at market prices (Cohen (2009), Benartzi and Thaler (2001)). In my framework, having some exposure to their employer firm's outcome insures against promotion risk.
  Second, nonexecutive workers tend to overvalue stock option pay (Oyer and Schaefer (2005), Hallock and Olson (2006), Hodge, Rajgopal, and Shevlin (2009)), while CEOs tend to undervalue it (Hall and Murphy (2002), Bettis, Bizjak, and Lemmon (2005)). The insurance benefit of firm performance pay may help explain the over- and undervaluation dichotomy of these observations. Nonexecutive workers who face promotion risk receive an insurance benefit from firm performance pay, which increases their subjective valuation of stock option pay. In contrast, the CEO derives no insurance benefit from stock option pay because the CEO is already at the top of the hierarchy and does not face promotion risk. The CEO simply values stock option pay from the perspective of an underdiversified investor, which leads to a subjective valuation below the fair market price.
  Prior studies propose separate explanations for each of these findings, typically relying on relaxed assumptions about rationality such as optimism (Oyer and Schaefer (2005), Hallock and Olson (2006), Bergman and Jenter (2007)), company loyalty (Cohen (2009)), loss aversion (Devers, Wiseman, and Holmes (2007)), and inexperience (Hallock and Olson (2006), Hodge, Rajgopal, and Shevlin (2009)). My framework provides a potential, unifying explanation for these findings based on the insurance role of firm performance pay.
  Related Literature
  Conventional analyses of promotion risk in large professional services firms focus on how firms can partially offset this risk with consolation prizes for qualified candidates who do not win the tournament. For instance, many professional services firms facilitate the outplacement of qualified associates who do not make partner (Gilson and Mnookin (1989), Malhotra, Morris, and Smets (2010)). In recent years, many firms have created new positions such as senior associate and nonequity partner that allow some losers of the promotion-to-partner tournament with high firm-specific human capital to stay at the firm (Galanter and Henderson (2007)). However, these consolation prizes only partially insure against promotion risk. Nonequity partners earn much less than true partners.6My framework complements these analyses and highlights how the firm can also insure against promotion risk using firm performance pay.
  More generally, this paper relates to the literature considering equity-type compensation for nonexecutive workers. Many studies emphasize financial constraints, noting that some firms simply cannot commit to paying fixed wages (e.g., Core and Guay (2001), Kim and Ouimet (2014), Sun and Xiaolan (2019), Efing et al. (2023)). Others focus on identifying a sufficiently large benefit to justify the cost of imposing risk on nonexecutive workers, who are presumably less diversified and more risk-averse than the firm. Some benefits include favorable accounting treatment (Hall and Murphy (2003)), employee retention (Oyer (2004), Kedia and Rajgopal (2009), Aldatmaz, Ouimet, and Van Wesep (2018), Lie and Que (2020), Hoffmann and Vladimirov (2023)), employee sorting (Lazear (2004), Bergman and Jenter (2007)), improved product market competitiveness (Bova and Yang (2017)), mutual monitoring and cooperation (Hochberg and Lindsey (2010)), and discouraging whistle-blowing (Call, Kedia, and Rajgopal (2016)). In my model, the firm does not face financial constraints.7Instead, it offers its workers firm performance pay as a form of insurance against promotion risk. I contribute to this literature by highlighting that firm performance pay can actually reduce a worker's payoff uncertainty in a setting with promotion risk.
  The idea that workers prefer pay that covaries with aggregate firm outcomes because it insures the workers against increased competition for promotions in the future is reminiscent of papers on “keeping up with the Joneses” (DeMarzo, Kaniel, and Kremer (2004,2007,2008)).8These studies argue that investors may prefer payouts that covary with aggregate wealth levels because such payouts hedge against increased competition over consumption and investment goods in the future. They investigate the asset pricing implications of investors' relative wealth concerns due to the competition for goods. In contrast, I study the optimal contracting implications of workers' relative performance concerns due to the competition for promotion.
  This paper also bridges the broad bodies of literature on optimal contracting and tournaments. Previous studies related to contracting and tournaments tend to consider each in isolation. Most dynamic contracting frameworks with career concerns do not feature promotion risk (e.g., Gibbons and Murphy (1992), Gibbons and Waldman (1999), Holmström (1999), Terviö (2009), Axelson and Bond (2015)). As a consequence, the insurance role that firm performance pay plays in my framework is absent in theirs. The tournament literature usually focuses on how to set up such a contest (e.g., Lazear and Rosen (1981), O'Keeffe, Viscusi, and Zeckhauser (1984), Lazear (1989), Chen (2003), Boudreau, Lakhani, and Menietti (2016)). While some authors compare contracts to tournaments (e.g., Lazear and Rosen (1981), Green and Stokey (1983), Nalebuff and Stiglitz (1983)), they rarely consider how the two interact. One notable recent exception is Ferreira and Nikolowa (2024), who study optimal career path design when some jobs are more prestigious than others. My study complements this literature by showing that the career path structure affects the compensation structure due to promotion risk.
  The remainder of the paper proceeds as follows. SectionIdescribes the model. SectionIIpresents the model's core results. SectionIIIanalyzes the model's comparative statics. SectionIVconsiders some extensions to the model. SectionVdiscusses the model's applications and empirical predictions. SectionVIconcludes.
article.I. Model Setup:
For simplicity, the worker's subjective discount factor,β$\beta$, is set to one. As long asβ>0$\beta >0$, its exact magnitude does not alter the main results of the paper.
  The insurance role of firm performance pay stems from how the worker's marginal utility of current pay changes with promotion risk. To capture this important property of economic behavior, namely how workers respond to news, I assume that workers face a consumption-smoothing problem att=1$t=1$, which induces a time-inseparable preference over pay.9Workers in earlier stages of their careers typically expect income to rise in the future, and thus, I impose parameter restrictions that result in equilibrium borrowing. For simplicity, I assume that workers do not face borrowing frictions.10The paper's conclusions do not depend on these restrictions. Firm performance pay can play an insurance role as long as workers borrow less (or save more) upon learning bad news about future promotion prospects.
  I describe the components of the model that result in promotion risk using the context of large professional services firms, where promotion-to-partner tournaments are the norm. SectionVdiscusses other interpretations. Production at the firm involves two types of tasks: lower level and higher level. For ease of exposition, I assume that the firm assigns its workers to lower level tasks in the first period (t=0$t=0$tot=1$t=1$) and to higher level tasks in the second period (t=1$t=1$tot=2$t=2$).
  The productivity of a worker depends on that worker's type, which can be high or low and is ex ante unknown to all. Uncertainty aboutΘ$\Theta$, the total mass of high-type workers at the firm, drives the uncertainty about promotion prospects in the model. The paper's main results hold for any nondegenerate distribution ofΘ$\Theta$(see SectionIV). To fix ideas, the model assumes that the firm assigns its workers evenly acrossn$n$projects, indexed byi$i$, with characteristicsνi∼i.i.dBernoulli(p)$\nu _i \overset{i.i.d}{\sim } Bernoulli(p)$. Each worker, indexed bya∈[0,1]$a \in [0,1]$, has underlying qualityηa∼i.i.dBernoulli(θ)$\eta _a \overset{i.i.d}{\sim } Bernoulli(\theta)$, whereθ$\theta$captures the average underlying quality of workers at the firm. To emphasize that worker type captures firm-specific human capital, I assume that a worker's type is given by the product of the two characteristicsνiηa$\nu _i \eta _a$. For instance, a large law firm may assign each associate to one of its major clients. An associate with high firm-specific human capital is someone with strong legal skills and the ability to gain the trust and respect of a major client. These assumptions imply thatΘ$\Theta$has the same distribution asθnN$\frac{\theta }{n}N$, whereN∼Binomial(n,p)$N \sim Binomial(n,p)$. For ease of exposition, I present the case withn=2$n=2$. In this case,Θ$\Theta$can take three values: high, low, and zero. For notational convenience, I refer to the high and low values asθH$\theta _H$andθL$\theta _L$, respectively.
  A worker conducting a lower level task generates outputq1a∈{0,xL,xH}$q_{1a} \in \lbrace 0,x_L,x_H\rbrace$that depends on that worker's type and efforte1a∈{0,1}$e_{1a} \in \lbrace 0,1\rbrace$. To emphasize the role of promotion risk, the model mutes other sources of uncertainty. First, workers learn their own types shortly after employment, prior to making the effort decision. This assumption is equivalent to the state-by-state incentive compatibility (IC) assumption of Edmans and Gabaix (2011). Second, given their effort decisions, workers face no uncertainty about their individual outputs in lower level tasks. A high-type worker generates high output (q1a=xH$q_{1a}=x_H$) with effort and no output without effort (q1a=0$q_{1a}=0$). Shirking by a high-type worker results in private benefits equivalent tob1H$b_{1H}$in income att=1$t=1$. A low-type worker generates low output (q1a=xL$q_{1a}=x_L$) with effort and no output(q1a=0)$(q_{1a}=0)$without effort. Shirking by a low-type worker results in private benefits equivalent tob1L$b_{1L}$in income att=1$t=1$. These assumptions allow the model to isolate the demand for insurance against promotion risk from the demand for insurance against other risks.11The paper's main results on the insurance role of firm performance pay are robust to these assumptions. The production technology for lower level tasks is summarized in TableI.
  The model aims to describe a setting in which the firm outcome provides little information about an individual worker's effort because it aggregates the output of many workers. For ease of exposition, the model captures this setting in the extreme by assuming a continuum of workers, which implies that the firm outcome provides no information about individual worker effort. Moreover, the firm outcome also provides no information about an individual worker's type over and above that contained in the worker's individual output. These features of the model help highlight the insurance role of firm performance pay and distinguish it from the informational role emphasized by Holmström (1979).
  While the firm's first-period outcome does not provide useful information about an individual worker's effort or type, it does provide information about the pool of promotion candidates. In particular, the first-period outcome provides a sufficient statistic for the mass of workers with high output in lower level tasks. Introducing noise into this signal of promotion prospects does not change the paper's main results (see SectionIV).
  In the second period, the firm can promote up to a fractionK∈(0,θ)$K \in (0,\theta)$of the workers from the first period to engage in higher-level tasks. In the main specification of the model,K$K$is fixed to capture the frictions that constrain the firm's ability to increase promotion opportunities. This assumption can be relaxed to allow for some firm growth.
  To capture the importance of firm-specific human capital in performing higher level tasks, I assume that these tasks are profitable for the firm only if high-type workers are assigned to them. Specifically, a low-type worker engaging in a higher level task generates no output and cannot extract private benefits. A high-type worker engaging in a higher level task generates high output (q2a=yH$q_{2a}=y_H$) with effort and no output (q2a=0$q_{2a}=0$) without effort. Shirking by a high-type worker engaging in a higher level task results in private benefits equivalent tob2H$b_{2H}$in income att=2$t=2$. The agency problem in higher level tasks allows the model to generate rents for promoted workers endogenously; one can generate higher pay for promoted workers in other ways without changing the paper's main results.12TableIIsummarizes the production technology for higher level tasks.
  In my framework, the promotion tournament plays dual roles, sorting workers and providing them with incentives. The model assumes that the firm promotes those it knows to be high-type workers (i.e., those with high output) with equal probability because they are identical.13The possibility of a promotion with increased pay in the second period provides implicit incentives to the workers in the first period. However, the inability of the firm to promote all high-output workers results in promotion risk.
  As is standard in the moral hazard literature, I assume that parameters are such that inducing effort is optimal for the firm. Hence, the objective of the firm is to induce effort at the lowest expected wage cost. Workers maximize their expected lifetime utility.
  The main specification of the model assumes that the firm enters into one-period contracts with its workers att=0$t=0$andt=1$t=1$. The assumption captures the difficulty of entering into long-term employment arrangements. The promotion-to-partner tournament is typically decade-long in large professional services firms.14SectionIVdiscusses how long-term contracting affects the insurance role of firm performance pay. A feasible contract att=0$t=0$, hereafter “the first-period contract,” specifies the payments to the worker att=1$t=1$given the worker's individual output and the firm outcome that period. Workers have an outside option att=0$t=0$that paysμ1$\mu _1$att=1$t=1$. The model assumes thatμ1$\mu _1$is sufficiently small such that a first-period contract that satisfies the worker's IC constraints also satisfies the worker's participation constraint. The promotion decision occurs aftert=1$t=1$payments are made. This timing assumption allows the model to capture the idea that it takes time for the firm to determine the suitability of a worker for a higher level task without adding an additional period.
  A feasible wage contract att=1$t=1$, hereafter “the second-period contract,” specifies the payments to the promoted worker att=2$t=2$given the worker's individual output and the firm outcome that period. The workers have an outside option att=1$t=1$that paysμ2$\mu _2$att=2$t=2$. The model assumes that parameters are such that a promotion pays better than no promotion. Workers are protected by limited liability, so payments to the worker cannot be negative. Figure1summarizes the timing of the model.
article.II. Optimal Contracts:
This section characterizes the optimal first- and second-period contracts. I proceed with backward induction, first solving for the optimal second-period contract,W2∗(q2,Q2)$W_2^*(q_2,Q_2)$, which specifies the payment to the promoted worker contingent on that worker's individual output and the firm outcome in the second period. I then solve for the optimal first-period contract,W1∗(q1,Q1)$W_1^*(q_1,Q_1)$, which specifies the payment to the worker contingent on that worker's individual output and the firm outcome in the first period, taking as given the promotion tournament due to the optimal second-period contract and the promotion rule. The characterization of the optimal contracts assumes that the firm makes take-it-or-leave-it contract offers. When the firm does not face financing constraints, improved bargaining power by the worker changes the division of the surplus but not the structure of the optimal contract. The worker subscript is suppressed for notational convenience.
  The optimal second-period contract follows from standard contracting arguments. The likelihood ratio for no output is zero. The optimal second-period contract sets the no-output payment at the minimum:W2∗(0,Q2)=0$W_2^*(0,Q_2)=0$for allQ2$Q_2$. The likelihood ratio for high output is infinite and the second-period firm outcome provides no incremental information about the worker's effort. The high-output payment binds (4) and does not depend on the firm outcome:W2∗(yH,Q2)=b2H$W_2^*(y_H,Q_2)=b_{2H}$for allQ2$Q_2$.
  In the model, promoted workers receive high pay due to the agency problem associated with higher level tasks. The paper's main results do not depend on the exact rationale behind the promoted worker's high pay; they only require that promoted workers receive higher pay than nonpromoted workers att=2$t=2$. To emphasize this point, I refer to thet=2$t=2$pay of promoted and nonpromoted workers simply asP1$P_1$andP0$P_0$, respectively. The pay differential,P1−P0$P_1-P_0$, serves as the prize for winning the promotion tournament.
  The possibility of promotion to a higher level task in the second period provides workers with implicit incentives in the first period. Exerting effort in lower level tasks allows high-type workers to credibly convey their types.
  Before solving for the optimal first-period contract, I eliminate an uninteresting case in which the implicit incentives from promotion alone are enough to induce effort from high-type workers in the first period. In this case, high-type workers exert effort in lower level tasks even if the first-period contract pays nothing for high output. The remainder of this paper focuses on the more interesting case in which the optimal first-period contract features positive high-output payments.
  Lemma 1.The optimal first-period contract pays the worker nothing for no output and a flat bonus for low individual output:
  
  
  The results of Lemma1follow from standard optimal contracting arguments. Only workers who shirk generate no output. Because the likelihood ratio for no output is zero, the optimal first-period contract sets the payment for no output at the minimum, which is zero due to limited liability. Low-type workers do not face promotion risk because they are not candidates for promotion. Hence, a bonus that is independent of the risky firm outcome minimizes the expected compensation of risk-averse, low-type workers.
  In contrast, high-type workers face promotion risk because there are a limited number of promotions. In some states of the world, they do not receive a promotion in the second period despite generating high output in the first period. The structure of the optimal first-period contract insures them against this risk.
  Proposition 1.When workers face promotion risk, the optimal first-period contract features a bonus for high individual output that increases with the firm outcome:
  
  
  The firm outcome provides a sufficient statistic for the mass of high-output workers, who make up the pool of candidates qualified for promotion. In particular, a better firm outcome in the first period corresponds to a larger pool of promotion candidates in the second period and hence to a lower promotion probability for any given high-output worker. When the firm outcome is high, the optimal first-period contract compensates the high-output worker for the decline in promotion prospects with an increase in pay.
  The insurance role of firm performance pay requires two elements. First, workers face promotion risk that increases with the firm outcome. Second, workers derive higher marginal utility from current pay upon learning bad news about future promotion prospects, which corresponds to a reduction in lifetime income.
  In contrast, my framework's high-output workers face promotion risk. In particular, they are less likely to be promoted in the second period if the firm outcome is high in the first period. In the model, workers face a consumption-smoothing problem at the end of the first period. The arrival of bad news about second-period promotion prospects makes first-period borrowing for consumption more costly because the corresponding second-period repayment will more likely occur when future income is low (i.e., after missing a promotion). Consequently, these workers derive additional benefits from first-period pay when the firm outcome is high and borrowing is effectively more costly. In other words, in my framework, the structure of optimal pay for high-output workers is driven by changes in the left-hand side of (11); the optimal first-period contract pays high-output workers more in states in which the marginal cost of transferring utility is lower.
article.III. Comparative Statics:
This section provides intuition for how the optimal first-period contract's firm performance pay sensitivity varies by deriving comparative statics of the model when workers have constant absolute risk aversion (CARA) utility over consumption:u(ct)=1−e−γctγ$u(c_t) = \tfrac{1-e^{-\gamma c_t}}{\gamma }$.
  
  Proposition 2.When workers have CARA utility, the firm performance pay sensitivity of the optimal first-period contract (Δ$\Delta$) has a nonmonotonic relationship withK$K$, increasing inK$K$whenK<θL$K<\theta _L$and decreasing inK$K$whenθL≤K<θH$\theta _L \le K < \theta _H$.
  
  Intuitively, an increase in promotion opportunities has two opposing effects on workers' exposure to promotion risk. A larger value ofK$K$increases the value of implicit incentives from promotions, which means that uncertainty about promotion prospects translates into greater income risk. However, an increase inK$K$also reduces the likelihood that a high-output worker is passed over for promotion. The first effect dominates when promotions are very scarce. The second effect dominates when promotions are less scarce. Figure2illustrates the nonmonotonic relationship betweenΔ$\Delta$andK$K$.
  Proposition 3.When workers have CARA utility, the firm performance pay sensitivity of the optimal first-period contract (Δ$\Delta$) has a nonmonotonic relationship withθ$\theta$, first increasing and then decreasing inθ$\theta$.
  
  Recall thatθ$\theta$captures the average underlying quality of workers at the firm. An increase inθ$\theta$has two opposing effects on firm performance pay sensitivity because it drives up both the mean and the variance ofΘ$\Theta$, the mass of high-type workers at the firm. A higher variance forΘ$\Theta$corresponds to more uncertainty about the pool of workers qualified for promotion, and more uncertain promotion prospects. At the same time, a higher mean forΘ$\Theta$implies a lower expected promotion probability, which decreases the value of implicit incentives from promotion. The variance effect dominates for small values ofθ$\theta$. The level effect dominates for larger ones. Figure3illustrates the nonmonotonic relationship betweenΔ$\Delta$andθ$\theta$.
  To further examine the effect of an increase in uncertainty aboutΘ$\Theta$on firm performance pay sensitivity, I provide comparative statics for the correlation parameterρ∈[0,1)$\rho \in [0,1)$whenΘ$\Theta$is given by a correlated binomial sum. A largerρ$\rho$corresponds to a higher correlation between the workers' types, making it more likely that the pool of qualified candidates is either very large or very small.
  Proposition 4.When workers have CARA utility, firm performance pay sensitivity of the optimal first-period contract (Δ$\Delta$) increases inρ$\rho$.
  
  A higher correlation between the workers' types implies that when a worker generates high output, other workers are also more likely to generate high output. In other words, an increase in the correlation between the workers' types increases their exposure to promotion risk, which results in more insurance provision via firm performance pay in the optimal first-period contract. In general, any change that results in the high-type worker's posterior ofΘ$\Theta$becoming first-order stochastically dominant increases the firm performance pay sensitivity of the optimal first-period contract.
  Proposition 5.When workers have CARA utility, the firm performance pay sensitivity of the optimal first-period contract (Δ$\Delta$) increases inP1$P_1$.
  
  Intuitively, higher pay for promoted workers makes the high-output worker's future income more sensitive to the promotion outcome, which increases the worker's exposure to promotion risk. The increased exposure results in additional insurance via firm performance pay in the optimal first-period contract, increasing its firm performance pay sensitivity. The opposite is true when better outside options for nonpromoted workers (i.e., higherμ2$\mu _2$) reduce the pay differential.
  Proposition 6.When workers have CARA utility and promotions are not too scarce (K≥θL$K \ge \theta _L$), the firm performance pay sensitivity of the optimal first-period contract (Δ$\Delta$) increases in the worker's relative risk aversion (γ$\gamma$).
  
  When promotions are not too scarce, firm performance pay sensitivity strictly increases in the worker's relative risk aversion, consistent with the insurance role of firm performance pay. Intuitively, the more risk-averse workers are, the more valuable insurance against promotion risk becomes. Hence, the firm performance pay sensitivity of the optimal first-period contract increases in the worker's risk aversion.
  It is worth noting that when promotions are very scarce (K<θL$K < \theta _L$), the relationship between the firm performance pay sensitivity of the optimal first-period contract and the worker's risk aversion is ambiguous. When promotions are very scarce, the high-output worker is never guaranteed a promotion. In this case, an increase in the worker's risk aversion also reduces the value of implicit incentives from promotion. Asγ$\gamma$goes to∞$\infty$, the high-output worker's certainty-equivalent second-period income goes toP0$P_0$, regardless of the firm outcome. In this limiting case, the possibility of promotion does not provide any implicit incentives and the optimal first-period contract does not feature firm performance pay. Hence, firm performance pay sensitivity must decline inγ$\gamma$for large enough values ofγ$\gamma$when promotions are very scarce.
article.IV. Extensions and Robustness:
The main specification of the model makes several simplifying assumptions to highlight how firm performance pay insures against promotion risk. In this section, I relax some of those assumptions and discuss how other economic forces and frictions in this setting affect the insurance role of firm performance pay.
  The main specification of the model assumes that the firm outcome is a deterministic function of aggregate worker performance. This assumption implies that the firm outcome provides a sufficient statistic for the promotion prospects of a high-output worker.
  The firm outcome may also be driven by factors that are unrelated to aggregate worker performance, such as macroeconomic and industry conditions. The firm may have many high-output workers but generate low profits due to weak customer demand. In this scenario, firm performance pay undercompensates high-output workers with poor promotion prospects. Alternatively, the firm may have few high-output workers but generate high profits due to strong customer demand. In this case, firm performance pay overcompensates high-output workers with excellent promotion prospects.
  The parameterε∈[0,1]$\epsilon \in [0,1]$captures the noisiness ofΠ1$\Pi _1$as a signal ofΘ$\Theta$. Whenε=0$\epsilon = 0$,Π1$\Pi _1$contains no noise and provides a sufficient statistic ofΘ$\Theta$just asQ1$Q_1$does in the main specification. Whenε=1$\epsilon = 1$,Π1$\Pi _1$contains only noise and provides no information aboutΘ$\Theta$.
  Proposition 7.If better firm outcomes correspond to more competition for promotion on average (i.e.,ε<1$\epsilon <1$), then the optimal first-period contract features firm performance pay as insurance against promotion risk.
  
  When the firm outcome only provides a noisy signal of promotion prospects, using firm performance pay to insure against promotion risk involves some basis risk, making it less attractive. However, as long as a better firm outcome corresponds to worse promotion prospects on average, firm performance pay continues to play an insurance role.
  Many frictions limit the growth in promotion opportunities (see SectionV). The main specification of the model captures the frictions that limit the growth in promotion opportunities by assuming that the number of higher level tasks is fixed by the firm's production technology. However, some firms may be able to increase promotion opportunities in order to accommodate a larger pool of high-type workers.
  I model this possibility by assuming that when the realized value ofΘ$\Theta$is high, the number of higher level tasks increases fromK$K$toKH≥K$K_H \ge K$. The main specification of the model corresponds toKH=K$K_H = K$.
  Proposition 8.If the firm's ability to increase promotion opportunities is limited, then the optimal first-period contract features firm performance pay as insurance against promotion risk:
  
  
  The upper bound on firm growth ensures that the high-output worker's promotion prospects decline when there is a larger pool of high-output workers. In other words, the firm performance pay result continues to hold as long as the growth in promotion opportunities cannot fully accommodate the increased supply of talent at the firm.
  The model can also incorporate growth in promotion opportunities due to a better firm outcome that is not necessarily driven by more high-output workers at the firm. Let the firm outcomeΠ1$\Pi _1$be a noisy signal ofΘ$\Theta$withε∈(0,1)$\epsilon \in (0,1)$as before. The number of promotions increases fromK$K$toKε≥K$K_\epsilon \ge K$when the firm outcome is high (i.e.,Π1=πH$\Pi _1 = \pi _H$).
  Corollary 1.If the ability of the firm to increase promotion opportunities is limited, then the optimal first-period contract features firm performance pay as insurance against promotion risk:
  
  
  Corollary1relies on the same logic underlying Propositions7and8. If the firm outcome contains some information about the mass of high-type workers at the firm, then a better firm outcome corresponds to more promotion candidates on average. As long as the increase in promotion opportunities due to a better firm outcome does not fully offset the expected increase in the number of promotion candidates, high-output workers have worse promotion prospects when the firm outcome is better. When the firm outcome is noisier, the expected increase in the number of promotion candidates is smaller, thereby lowering the growth required to fully offset the increased promotion competition.
  One prominent explanation of nonexecutive pay based on risky firm outcomes is the wage-indexation theory of Oyer (2004). Oyer (2004, p. 1623) notes that “other firms infer the firm's workers' average ability from [the firm outcome].” When their employer performs well, workers have better outside options, resulting in higher pay. Many empirical studies document evidence consistent with this explanation (Oyer and Schaefer (2006), Kedia and Rajgopal (2009), Aldatmaz, Ouimet, and Van Wesep (2018)).
  To see how such variation in a worker's outside option affects the insurance role of firm performance pay, suppose a nonpromoted high-output worker's pay in the second period increases fromP0$P_0$toP0+δ0$P_0 + \delta _0$when the first-period firm outcome is high.
  Proposition 9.If the increase in the nonpromoted high-output worker's second-period outside option due to a better first-period firm outcome is limited, then the optimal first-period contract features firm performance pay as insurance against promotion risk:
  
  
  In general, any improvement in the nonpromoted worker's outside option in the second period mitigates promotion risk because it makes the high-type worker's second-period income less dependent on the promotion outcome. An improved outside option, particularly when the firm performs better, also weakens the negative relationship between the firm outcome and promotion prospects. When the firm performs better, the high-output worker is more likely to be passed over for promotion. However, the high-output worker also receives more pay if not promoted. In other words, when the high-output worker's second-period outside option improves with the first-period firm outcome, that worker is exposed to an outside option risk that offsets promotion risk. The optimal first-period contract features firm performance pay as long as promotion risk dominates.
  When workers have CARA utility over consumption (i.e.,u(ct)=1−e−γctγ$u(c_t) = \tfrac{1-e^{-\gamma c_t}}{\gamma }$), one can derive an exact expression forδ¯0$\bar{\delta }_0$, which allows for additional analysis.
  Proposition 10.When workers have CARA utility over consumption,δ¯0$\bar{\delta }_0$increases inP1$P_1$, and decreases inP0$P_0$andΦH$\Phi _H$.
  
  Intuitively, an increase in the pay differential, due to either a largerP1$P_1$or a smallerP0$P_0$, makes the high-output worker's second-period income more sensitive to the promotion outcome. A smallerΦH$\Phi _H$implies that an adverse promotion outcome is more likely when the firm outcome is high. As a result, a larger outside option risk (i.e., higherδ0$\delta _0$) is necessary to fully offset the worker's exposure to promotion risk.
  It is worth noting that wage-indexation models and my framework describe different mechanisms for different components of pay. In a wage-indexation model, a better first-period firm outcome signals higher average worker ability and raises the second-period outside option ofallworkers at the firm. Hence, all workers receive second-period pay that increases with the first-period firm outcome to account for their higher outside option. In my framework, a better first-period firm outcome signals worse promotion prospects only for high-output workers; low-output workers are never candidates for promotion. Hence, only high-output workers receive first-period pay that increases with the firm outcome as insurance against second-period promotion risk.
  For ease of exposition, the main specification focuses on the case in whichn=2$n=2$. WhenΘ$\Theta$can take on more values (i.e.,n≥3$n \ge 3$), the model also generates additional predictions about the structure of firm performance pay. SupposeΘ$\Theta$takes values 0,θ1$\theta _1$,θ2$\theta _2$,…,θn$\theta _n$with probabilitiesp0$p_0$,p1$p_1$,…,pn$p_n$, respectively, where theθi$\theta _i$values are positive and increase ini$i$. LetQ1i$Q_{1i}$be the first-period firm outcome when all workers exert effort and the realized value ofΘ$\Theta$isθi$\theta _i$:Q1i=xL+θi(xH−xL)$Q_{1i} = x_L + \theta _i (x_H -x_L)$.
  Proposition 11.When the optimal first-period contract features positive payments for high output, these bonuses increase with the firm outcome after a threshold:
  
  
  Recall that the firm outcomeQ1$Q_1$provides a sufficient statistic for the pool of candidates qualified for promotion. WhenQ1≤xL+K(xH−xL)$Q_1 \le x_L + K(x_H-x_L)$, there are enough higher -level tasks for all high-output workers to receive a promotion. In such instances, high-output workers do not face promotion risk. Hence, the bonus for high output does not increase with the firm outcome in this lower range. WhenQ1>xL+K(xH−xL)$Q_1 > x_L + K(x_H-x_L)$, there are more high-output workers than there are higher level tasks. Hence, once the firm outcome exceeds this threshold, the optimal first-period contract pays the high-output worker more to offset the effects of declining promotion prospects.
  This threshold characteristic does not rely on the number of higher level tasks being fixed. Instead, it only requires that a lower range of firm outcomes corresponds to all high-output workers being promoted. The possibility ofK$K$increasing with the firm outcome simply moves the threshold.
  The model's main specification focuses on one-period contracts to emphasize the difficulty of establishing long-term contractual arrangements. In this section, I discuss an extension that allows the firm to offer two-period contracts att=0$t=0$that specify the payments to the worker att=1$t=1$andt=2$t=2$. The main difference is that a two-period contract allows the firm to commit to making a bonus payment to high-output workers who are not promoted in the second period. I assume that the promised payments att=2$t=2$are made with probabilityτ∈[0,1]$\tau \in [0,1]$. The parameterτ$\tau$captures the firm's ability to commit to long-term contractual arrangements. Whenτ=0$\tau =0$, the firm can only write one-period contracts as in the main specification. At the other extreme, whenτ=1$\tau =1$, the firm can fully commit to making payments att=2$t=2$. This section focuses on the intuition of this extension; the details are relegated to theInternet Appendix.
  With long-term contracting, the firm can insure a worker against promotion risk using firm performance pay in the first period and promotion-contingent bonuses in the second period. Firm performance pay only indirectly insures against the adverse promotion outcome. For instance, the high-output worker may receive low firm performance pay in the first period and still miss out on a promotion in the second period. In contrast, a bonus in the second period contingent on not being promoted directly insures against the adverse promotion outcome. When the firm can guarantee second-period payments (i.e.,τ=1$\tau =1$), direct insurance via promotion-contingent bonuses dominates indirect insurance via firm performance pay. In this case, the optimal two-period contract insures against promotion risk only via promotion-contingent payments in the second period.
  When the firm cannot fully guarantee future payments (i.e.,τ<1$\tau < 1$), it cannot completely shield workers from adverse promotion outcomes in the second period. The workers are exposed to residual promotion risk, which increases with the firm outcome. In this case, first-period firm performance pay can continue to play an insurance role alongside second-period promotion-contingent bonuses in the optimal two-period contract.
article.V. Applications:
The insurance role of firm performance pay relies on a negative relationship between the firm's performance and its workers' promotion prospects. In the model, this negative relationship is driven by thesupplyof high-performing workers at the firm. More high-performing workers improve the firm outcome but also intensify the competition for promotion. At the same time, a firm with better outcomes may expand promotion opportunities, either to accommodate an abundance of talent or to satisfy an increase in the demand for its goods and services. The firm's competitors may also be more likely to poach its workers following strong performance, mitigating its workers' exposure to promotion risk. In general, factors that increase thedemandfor high-performing workers undermine the negative relationship between firm outcome and promotion prospects.17
  Certainly, demand factors play an important role in shaping worker income. Cowgill (2015) reports that many of the 15,540 respondents to a Google Consumer Survey said their most recent pay raise resulted from various improvements to labor market conditions.18However, a plurality (41.6%$41.6\%$) identify “Performing well compared to peers at job” as the source of their most recent pay increase, suggesting that the supply effect emphasized in my framework is also relevant.
  Propositions8and10imply that the supply effect dominates those of demand when organizational frictions sufficiently constrain the ability of the firm to increase promotion opportunities for its workers. These frictions are particularly relevant for management positions. Accommodating an additional qualified management candidate involves much more than adding one position; it requires the creation of an entire team, department, or division. As Miller and Friesen (1984, p. 1171) point out in their seminal work on organizational life cycles, mature firms “are conservative […] engage in very few efforts at diversification or acquisition […] and fail to even make many incremental changes to the products or services being offered” (see also Lester, Parnell, and Carraher (2003)). There are also many other economic rationales for limiting firm expansion, such as decreasing returns to scale, the need to signal quality (Levin and Tadelis (2005)), and limited monitoring capacity at the top (Oi (1983)). Anecdotally, large firms also worry about collusion. HR professionals interviewed by Cowgill (2015, p. 7) note that “if we didn't limit promotions, then managers would simply promote everyone, as a means of buying the cooperation and loyalty of their workers at the expense of the company.”
  Not surprisingly, the aggregate promotion rates for each level tend to correspond to the growth patterns for the company as a whole, albeit not perfectly. Between 1962–65 and 1965–69, when the company experienced increasing growth, promotion rates increased for the two lower levels, but not for lower management. (p. 36)
  Treble et al. (2001) study the personnel history of a large U.K. financial services firm over a period of decline and report that the proportion of workers in management grades increased from23%$23\%$to31%$31\%$of total employment; those in senior management grades increased from5%$5\%$to8%$8\%$. The authors note that the increase is driven by a significant reduction in the number of lower level positions. This body of evidence suggests that firm expansion does not always increase promotion opportunities for workers in the middle of the hierarchy. Instead, increased hiring in lower levels may further intensify subsequent promotion competition for limited management positions.
  Propositions7and8emphasize that the insurance role of firm performance pay is more pronounced when firm outcomes strongly correlate with overall worker performance. This paper focuses on large professional services firms because they rely on their workers' human capital to generate output and also face significant organizational frictions, which result in explicit promotion-to-partner tournaments.
  One can also apply the model's results to mid-level employees in large, knowledge-intensive corporations. While large corporations have many job levels, organizational frictions often result in a bottleneck in the middle of the hierarchy (Baker, Gibbs, and Holmstrom (1994), Treble et al. (2001)). For example, Baker, Gibbs, and Holmstrom (1994) study the 20-year personnel history of a medium-sized U.S. financial services firm and provide a simple characterization of its eight-level hierarchy. Employees in Levels 1 to 4 carry out a combination of “line” work, which involves the creating and selling of products and services, and general administration (i.e., lower level tasks). Employees in Levels 5 to 8 perform work associated with management and planning (i.e., higher level tasks). Hence, my framework also describes the transition of white-collar workers from the bottom half to the top half of the firm hierarchy.
  White-collar workers in large corporations frequently encounter explicit promotion tournament policies. The common practice of forced ranking, popularized by Jack Welch during his time as CEO of General Electric, ranks employee performances and rewards a fixed percentage of top performers with promotions and raises and punishes bottom performers. For instance, Yahoo allocated 10% of its white-collar employees into “greatly exceeds,” 25% into “exceeds,” and so forth on a quarterly basis; those not ranked highly lost out on promotions (Carlson (2015)).19The large technology firm studied by Cowgill (2015) explicitly limits the number of promotions in each business unit.20Despite recent controversies about the practice, forced ranking remains popular among large firms, with an estimated 60% of Fortune 500 companies using some form of employee ranking.21
  One may naturally wonder how the model applies in a setting in which demand factors dominate. For instance, young firms such as start-ups face fewer organizational frictions and may grow when they perform well. The logic underlying Proposition1implies that when a high-output worker's promotion prospects improve with the firm outcome, insurance against promotion risk involves a larger bonus when the firm performs poorly.22However, insurance against promotion risk is less relevant in this alternative setting.
  First, young firms are less able to provide insurance because they likely face binding cash constraints when they fare poorly.23The prevailing wisdom warns start-ups against paying bonuses, especially after a poor performance, as such bonuses use up much-needed cash, disincentivize cooperation, damage theesprit de corp, etc.24Second, existing workers at young firms may not have the human capital required to fill the new positions created by growth. Babina, Ouimet, and Zarutskie (2022) document that young firms experience large increases in employment after an initial public offering (IPO), but note that the increase in labor rents accrues to those hired after the IPO. Taken together, these factors suggest that my model does not generate strong predictions for the compensation practices of young firms.
  The optimal first-period contract in my framework has two distinguishing characteristics. First, it features vesting based on individual performance; only high-output workers receive pay that increases with the firm outcome (Proposition1). Second, pay for high output increases with the firm outcome only after a threshold (Proposition11). These features are consistent with the common practice of profit-sharing for top performers in large professional services firms. For example, the website for Reed Smith LLP, a large U.S. law firm, notes that its associates who generate more than 2,000 billable hours are eligible for a bonus payment, the size of which depends on the overall profitability of the firm.25More broadly, compensation plans for mid-level workers at large corporations also routinely feature year-end bonuses based on two multipliers with minimum thresholds: one based on individual performance, the other based on a firm outcome.
  The discussion in SectionIVimplies that the optimal long-term contract features promotion-contingent bonuses. In general, it is difficult for firms to enter into long-term contractual arrangements, limiting the viability of explicit promises to pay bonuses to workers who are passed over for promotion. However, some compensation practices may serve the same purpose. For example, Jokinen and Pehkonen (2021, p. 494) investigate the personnel history of a large Finnish institution and find evidence consistent with seniority bonuses being used to “give salary increases to more senior employees who had performed well over the longer term, but who had not been promoted to higher-paying job levels.”26These instances lend further support to the idea that promotion risk is a relevant source of uncertainty for many workers.
  Conventional contracting frameworks view pay based on risky firm outcomes as the result of an optimal trade-off between the benefit of providing incentives and the cost of imposing risk. Yet workers seem to demand some exposure to their employer's risky firm outcomes. Kruse, Blasi, and Park (2010) analyze a series of surveys sponsored by the National Bureau of Economic Research (NBER) and report that78%$78\%$of workers, including66%$66\%$of the most risk-averse, indicate that they prefer their compensation to include some form of shared capitalism (e.g., stock, profit-sharing). As Kruse, Blasi, and Park (2010, p. 64) note, these observations “would not make sense if the employees were seeing the shared capitalism as simply adding risk.”
  In addition, many workers voluntarily buy shares in their employer at market prices. Benartzi and Thaler (2001) investigate 170 retirement savings plans with 1.56 million participants and note that when given the option to do so, the participants allocate42%$42\%$of their portfolio to their employer's equity (see also Cohen (2009)). Moreover, Benartzi and Thaler (2001) show that these participants do not view equity in other firms as a substitute for equity in their employer. These observations suggest that workers perceive some unique benefits of having exposure to their employer's firm outcome. This paper argues that one of these benefits is insurance against promotion risk. Cohen (2009) studies of company loyalty and provides some evidence consistent with this insurance role. For instance, he reports that workers who are less exposed to promotion risk (e.g., union workers, hourly workers) allocate less of their portfolio to their employer's equity.
  In contrast to conventional contracting frameworks, my model predicts that a worker's risk aversion and the use of firm performance pay may be positively correlated in some cases (Proposition6). Kruse, Blasi, and Park (2010) provide mixed evidence, reporting that more risk-averse workers are more likely to participate in some forms of shared capitalism (e.g., profit-sharing) but less likely for others (e.g., stock ownership). A more direct test of the prediction would examine the relationship controlling for promotion opportunities.
  The benefit of insurance against promotion risk may also help explain why nonexecutive workers and CEOs value the stock options in their pay packages differently. Nonexecutive workers tend to value the stock options in their compensation above fair-market prices. For instance, Hodge, Rajgopal, and Shevlin (2009) find that lower level managers require an additional $38,688 in cash wages to forgo stock options in their pay package with a Black-Scholes value of $30,000 (see also Oyer and Schaefer (2005), Hallock and Olson (2006)). In stark contrast to these findings for nonexecutive workers, Bettis, Bizjak, and Lemmon (2005) estimate that CEOs and board members value the stock options in their compensation at about 20% less than Black-Scholes value (see also Hall and Murphy (2002)).
  In my framework, nonexecutive workers who face promotion risk derive an insurance benefit from stock options in their employer. This insurance benefit increases their subjective valuation of stock option pay. Unlike nonexecutive workers, individuals at the top of the corporate hierarchy are much less exposed to promotion risk. After all, the CEO cannot be promoted again at the same company. Consequently, CEOs and board members, who are underdiversified in firm-specific risks relative to the marginal investor, value the stock options in their pay at less than the fair-market price.
  Empirical studies document a positive relationship between a firm's stock return volatility and its propensity to offer stock option pay to its nonexecutive workers (Oyer and Schaefer (2005), Spalt (2013)). Most principal-agent frameworks with risk-averse agents predict a negative relationship because an increase in the firm's stock return volatility makes stock option pay more costly. One notable exception is the wage-indexation theory of Oyer (2004) that predicts that a firm is more likely to use stock option pay when thesystematiccomponent of its stock return volatility is higher, consistent with the findings of Oyer and Schaefer (2005).
  My framework complements the analysis of Oyer (2004) by highlighting how insurance against promotion risk can result in a positive link between a firm's use of stock option pay and the component of its risk due to the uncertain quality of its workers' human capital (Proposition4). Spalt (2013) offers consistent evidence showing that a firm is also more likely to use stock option pay when theidiosyncraticcomponent of its stock return volatility is higher and that this relationship is more pronounced for new economy firms, which are more likely to rely on their workers' human capital to generate value.
  Proposition7implies that firm performance pay should be more prevalent in industries where the firm outcome strongly correlates with aggregate worker performance. Kedia and Rajgopal (2009) investigate stock option pay for nonexecutive workers and provide some supportive evidence. They report that the two industries that grant the most stock option pay to nonexecutive workers are Business Services (SIC 73) and Engineering and Management Services (SIC 87); the performance of a firm in these industries should correlate strongly with aggregate worker performance (see also Spalt (2013)). They also report that the Electronic and Other Electrical Equipment, Except Computer Equipment (SIC 36) industry grants the third-most stock option pay, which suggests that stock option grants also play other roles. Cohen (2009) reports that workers in diversified conglomerates are less likely to invest in company stock than workers in standalone firms. If promotion decisions are segmented at the business unit level, then the firm-level outcome of a diversified conglomerate provides a noisier signal of promotion prospects.
  My framework suggests that labor market changes that result in a greater pay differential between promoted and nonpromoted workers amplify promotion risk, thereby making the insurance role of firm performance pay more relevant (see Propositions5,9, and10). Many studies find that increased labor market competitiveness correlates with greater use of stock option pay (Kedia and Rajgopal (2009), Aldatmaz, Ouimet, and Van Wesep (2018), Lie and Que (2020)). However, interpreting these findings within my framework poses challenges.
  First, increased labor market competition has dual effects on the pay differential. A more competitive labor market may help nonpromoted workers transition to better paying jobs elsewhere. At the same time, retaining promoted workers becomes more challenging, potentially increasing their compensation as well. For example, studies of large law firms note that the pay for equity partners must be high enough to dissuade them from moving to rival firms with major clients in tow (e.g., Galanter and Henderson (2007)).
  Second, besides linking compensation to firm performance, stock option pay also defers payment across a vesting period that spans multiple years. Because workers receive the stock options only if they remain with the firm, the deferred nature of stock option pay also contributes to worker retention (e.g., Aldatmaz, Ouimet, and Van Wesep (2018), Jochem, Ladika, and Sautner (2018), Lie and Que (2020)), which is more important in competitive labor markets.
  A more direct test of the insurance role of firm performance pay would examine whether labor market conditions that increase the pay differential also result in more nondeferred firm performance pay like profit-sharing and annual firm performance bonuses.
  My model generates a number of novel testable implications that link the firm's hierarchy to its compensation policy. First, Proposition2highlights a nonmonotonic relation between the firm performance pay sensitivity and the ratio of higher level to lower level tasks. Proposition3predicts a similar nonmonotonic relationship between firm performance pay sensitivity and the average underlying quality of workers at the firm. Second, if nonpromoted workers stay at their existing job level, then the pay differential also captures the difference in pay between organizational levels. Proposition5would then predict more firm performance pay sensitivity when the wage gap between organizational levels is greater. Finally, Proposition11implies that the threshold for firm performance pay should be positively associated with the expected vacancy in higher level positions. To my knowledge, no empirical study has examined these predictions.
article.VI. Conclusion:
This paper analyzes a principal-agent problem in a setting with promotion tournaments, which are common in the workplace. When workers compete against each other for a limited number of promotions, they face promotion risk, the possibility of missing out on a promotion despite having performed at a high level. A larger number of high-performing workers improves the firm outcome but also increases the competition for promotion. When promotion risk increases with the firm outcome, the optimal contract features firm performance pay as a form of insurance.
  Organizational frictions that hinder the creation of additional promotion opportunities when the firm does well drive the negative relationship between the firm's performance and its workers' promotion prospects. Such frictions, therefore, play a critical role in allowing firm performance pay to insure against promotion risk. Firm performance pay makes nonexecutive workers residual claimants to some of the firm's value. Empirical studies in the finance literature show that equity-type pay for nonexecutive workers affects many corporate policies such as capital structure (McKeon (2015)), innovation and investment (Babenko, Lemmon, and Tserlukevich (2011), Chang et al. (2015)), and payout (Babenko (2009)). Consequently, this paper highlights an underexplored connection between organizational economics and topics in finance.
  Finally, this paper also contributes to the study of optimal contracting when workers have time-inseparable preferences over pay. The insurance role of firm performance pay relies on the link between future promotion prospects and the marginal utility of current pay. Without such a link, workers would derive no insurance benefits from higher pay upon learning bad news about future promotion prospects. This result suggests that further work in contracting with an emphasis on time-inseparability may uncover other economic forces overlooked by conventional studies.
